expanded_description: A clear introduction to bacterial genetics for secondary school biology. This covers how bacteria store their genetic information, how they copy it, how they get new genes through mutation and horizontal gene transfer, and how this allows them to adapt quickly—including the spread of antibiotic resistance.

core_concept.principle: Bacteria have a single circular chromosome that contains their essential genes. They can also have small circles of DNA called plasmids that carry extra genes, like antibiotic resistance. Bacteria share genes through horizontal gene transfer—transformation (taking up DNA from the environment), transduction (DNA carried by viruses), and conjugation (direct transfer between cells).
core_concept.explanation:
Bacteria are simple, but their genetics is fascinating. They have a single, circular chromosome that holds all the genes they need to live. This chromosome is about 4-5 million base pairs long—enough to code for about 4,000 proteins. The DNA is not in a nucleus; it floats in the cytoplasm in a region called the nucleoid.

In addition to the chromosome, many bacteria have plasmids. Plasmids are small circles of DNA, usually 1,000 to 200,000 base pairs long. They carry extra genes—genes that are helpful in certain environments but not essential for life. Antibiotic resistance genes are often on plasmids. This is important because plasmids can be copied and passed to other bacteria.

Bacteria reproduce by binary fission. Before dividing, they copy their DNA. The copying starts at a specific spot called oriC. Two copies are made, one for each daughter cell. The copying is very accurate—mistakes happen only about once in a billion base pairs. Bacteria have proofreading enzymes that fix mistakes as they copy, and repair enzymes that fix damage later.

Sometimes mistakes happen anyway. These are mutations. A mutation can change a gene. Sometimes the change is harmful, sometimes it has no effect, and occasionally it is helpful. If a helpful mutation helps the bacterium survive antibiotics, that bacterium will grow and pass the mutation to its offspring.

Bacteria can also get new genes from other bacteria. This is called horizontal gene transfer. There are three ways this happens.

Transformation: Bacteria take up DNA from their environment. If a bacterium dies and breaks open, its DNA spills out. Other bacteria can pick up this DNA. If the DNA has a useful gene (like antibiotic resistance), the recipient bacterium can incorporate it into its own DNA.

Transduction: Viruses that infect bacteria (bacteriophages) sometimes accidentally pick up bacterial DNA instead of viral DNA. When the virus infects another bacterium, it injects that bacterial DNA. The new bacterium gets new genes.

Conjugation: Bacteria form a bridge (pilus) between two cells. One cell (the donor) copies a plasmid and sends the copy through the bridge to the other cell (the recipient). The recipient now has the plasmid. This is how antibiotic resistance spreads so quickly—a resistant bacterium can give a copy of its resistance plasmid to a sensitive one in minutes.

Understanding bacterial genetics helps us understand how bacteria evolve, how they become resistant to antibiotics, and how we can use them in biotechnology.

image.alt: A diagram showing a bacterial cell with a circular chromosome and plasmids, and arrows for transformation (DNA uptake), transduction (virus), and conjugation (pilus transfer)
image.caption: Bacterial genetics: Chromosome (essential genes) + Plasmids (extra genes, like antibiotic resistance). Horizontal gene transfer: Transformation (free DNA), Transduction (virus), Conjugation (pilus transfer).

sections.introduction:
Every living thing has a blueprint—a set of instructions that tells it how to grow, reproduce, and respond to the environment. For bacteria, this blueprint is DNA. It is a single, circular molecule that contains all the genes the bacterium needs to survive.

Bacterial DNA is simple compared to ours. Humans have 23 pairs of linear chromosomes—46 in total. Bacteria have one circular chromosome. It is about 4 million base pairs long. That is much shorter than the human genome (3 billion base pairs), but it is enough for about 4,000 genes.

Bacteria also have small, circular DNA molecules called plasmids. Plasmids are not essential for life, but they carry genes that can be very helpful. Some plasmids carry genes for antibiotic resistance. Others carry genes for making toxins or for breaking down unusual foods. Plasmids can be copied and passed to other bacteria.

Bacteria reproduce by binary fission. Before dividing, they must copy their DNA. This is called replication. The process starts at a specific spot on the chromosome called oriC (origin of replication). Two copies are made, and each daughter cell gets one copy.

Copying DNA is not perfect. Mistakes happen. These mistakes are called mutations. Most mutations are harmful, but a few can be helpful. For example, a mutation that makes a bacterium resistant to penicillin could help it survive when penicillin is present.

Bacteria can also get new genes from other bacteria. This is called horizontal gene transfer. They can pick up DNA from dead bacteria (transformation). They can get DNA from viruses (transduction). They can even swap DNA directly with living bacteria (conjugation). This is how antibiotic resistance spreads so quickly—a resistant bacterium can share its resistance genes with its neighbors.

Understanding bacterial genetics helps us understand how bacteria evolve, how they become resistant to antibiotics, and how we can use them to make medicines and other products.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram of a bacterial cell with a circular chromosome and several plasmids
introduction_image.caption: Bacterial DNA: A single circular chromosome (essential genes) and small plasmids (extra genes, like antibiotic resistance). Plasmids can be shared between bacteria.

key_concept.definition: Bacteria have a circular chromosome with essential genes. They may also have plasmids—small DNA circles with extra genes. They share genes through transformation (taking up free DNA), transduction (virus transfer), and conjugation (direct cell-to-cell transfer).
key_concept.context: The chromosome is copied before cell division. Mutations happen occasionally. Some mutations help bacteria survive. Horizontal gene transfer lets bacteria share helpful genes, including antibiotic resistance. This is why resistance spreads so quickly.

image.alt: Labeled diagram: chromosome, plasmids, replication fork, transformation, transduction, conjugation
image.caption: Bacterial genetics: Chromosome (essential), Plasmids (extra), Replication (copying DNA), Transformation (uptake of free DNA), Transduction (virus transfer), Conjugation (cell-to-cell transfer).

sections.key_concept: Bacteria store their genes in a circular chromosome and sometimes in plasmids. They copy their DNA accurately before dividing. Mutations create new gene variants. Horizontal gene transfer (transformation, transduction, conjugation) lets bacteria share genes with each other.

classifications.value: Understanding bacterial DNA explains how they store information and why they can share genes so easily.
characteristics: Chromosome: A single circular DNA molecule. Contains essential genes (about 4,000 in E. coli). Located in the nucleoid (no nuclear membrane).
Plasmids: Small circular DNA molecules, separate from the chromosome. Carry non-essential genes that can be helpful (antibiotic resistance, toxin production, etc.).
Replication: DNA copying starts at oriC and proceeds in both directions. Takes about 40 minutes in E. coli.
Fidelity: Mistakes happen about once in a billion bases. Proofreading and repair keep errors low.
Multifork replication: When bacteria grow very fast, they start new copies before finishing old ones.
classifications.behavior:
The bacterial chromosome is a single loop of DNA. If you stretched it out, it would be about 1.5 mm long—1,000 times longer than the bacterium itself. It is packed into the nucleoid by special proteins that twist and fold it.

Before a bacterium divides, it must copy its DNA. The process starts at a specific spot called oriC. Two replication forks move in opposite directions around the circle, copying the DNA as they go. It takes about 40 minutes to copy the whole E. coli chromosome.

If E. coli is growing very fast (dividing every 20 minutes), it cannot wait 40 minutes to finish copying before dividing. Instead, it starts new copies before the old ones are finished. This is called multifork replication. At any moment, the cell has several copies of the DNA in progress.

Plasmids are like tiny extra chromosomes. They are usually much smaller—1,000 to 200,000 base pairs. They have their own origin of replication and can be copied independently of the chromosome. This is important because plasmids can be shared between bacteria. A resistant bacterium can make many copies of its resistance plasmid and give them to neighbors.

examples.process: How E. coli Copies Its DNA
examples.explanation:
E. coli's chromosome is about 4.6 million base pairs long. Copying it takes about 40 minutes. The process is very accurate—mistakes happen only about once in a billion bases. How do they do it?

First, the DNA starts at a specific spot called oriC. Proteins bind here and open the DNA, creating a bubble. Then, enzymes called helicases unwind the DNA, and primases lay down RNA primers. DNA polymerases add new bases to the growing strands. One strand is copied continuously (leading strand), the other in pieces (lagging strand).

The DNA polymerases have a proofreading function. When they add a base, they check it. If it is wrong, they remove it and try again. This catches most mistakes. After replication, another set of enzymes checks for any mistakes that got through. If they find a mismatch, they cut out the error and fill it in correctly.

The result is an error rate of about 1 mistake per 10 billion bases. This is very good—but not perfect. The few mistakes that slip through are mutations.

image.alt: DNA replication: oriC, two replication forks moving opposite directions, leading and lagging strands
image.caption: DNA replication: Starts at oriC. Two replication forks move in opposite directions. Leading strand (continuous), lagging strand (Okazaki fragments). Proofreading fixes most mistakes.

examples.explanation:
Plasmids are small circles of DNA that float freely in the cell. They are not essential—a bacterium can live without them. But they can carry genes that give the bacterium an advantage in certain environments.

Some plasmids carry antibiotic resistance genes. If a bacterium has a plasmid with a gene for resistance to ampicillin, it can grow in ampicillin. If a neighboring bacterium does not have the plasmid, it will die. The resistant bacterium can copy its plasmid and give a copy to the sensitive neighbor (conjugation). Soon, the whole population is resistant.

Other plasmids carry genes for making toxins. The bacteria that cause anthrax and botulism get their toxin genes from plasmids. Some plasmids carry genes for breaking down unusual foods—like oil or plastic.

Plasmids can be transferred between bacteria, even between different species. This is how antibiotic resistance spreads so quickly. A resistance plasmid that evolves in one species can jump to another species, creating new resistant strains.

image.alt: Plasmids in a bacterium, showing antibiotic resistance genes, and transfer between bacteria
image.caption: Plasmids: Small DNA circles with extra genes (antibiotic resistance, toxins, metabolism). Can be transferred between bacteria. Major vehicles for spreading resistance.

classifications.value: Understanding mutations explains how bacteria evolve and how resistance arises.
characteristics: Point mutations: Change in a single base. Can be silent (no change in protein), missense (one amino acid changed), or nonsense (stop codon early).
Insertions/deletions: Add or remove bases. Often cause frameshifts (reading frame shifts), usually lethal.
Mutation rate: About 1 mistake per billion bases per replication. In E. coli, about 1 mutation per 1,000 cells per generation.
Causes: Replication errors, chemicals, radiation (UV), transposons (jumping genes).
Selection: Mutations that help bacteria survive (like antibiotic resistance) spread rapidly.
classifications.behavior:
Mutations happen. Even with proofreading and repair, some mistakes slip through. In E. coli, about 1 mutation occurs in every 1,000 cells per generation. This sounds like a lot, but most mutations are harmful or neutral. Only a few are helpful.

A helpful mutation might make a bacterium resistant to an antibiotic. For example, a mutation in the gene for the ribosome might prevent streptomycin from binding. The mutant bacteria survive when streptomycin is present. They grow and divide, passing the mutation to their offspring. Soon, the whole population is resistant.

Mutations are random. They do not happen because the bacterium needs them. But if a mutation happens to be helpful, it will spread. This is natural selection—the same process that shapes all evolution.

Bacteria can also increase their mutation rate in times of stress. When DNA is damaged (by UV light or antibiotics), the SOS response is activated. The bacterium makes error-prone polymerases that copy damaged DNA, causing many mutations. This is risky—most mutations are harmful—but it can create new traits that help the bacterium survive. It also can create resistance to the antibiotic that caused the damage.

examples.process: How Bacteria Become Resistant to Rifampicin
examples.explanation:
Rifampicin is an antibiotic that kills bacteria by blocking RNA polymerase. RNA polymerase is the enzyme that makes RNA from DNA. If RNA polymerase is blocked, the bacterium cannot make proteins and dies.

But sometimes, a mutation changes the RNA polymerase so that rifampicin no longer binds. The mutation changes one amino acid in the protein. The enzyme still works, but the drug cannot attach. The bacterium survives.

This mutation happens randomly, about once in 100 million cells. If a patient has a large bacterial population (10⁹ cells), there are probably about 10 resistant cells already present before treatment starts. When the patient takes rifampicin, the susceptible cells die. The resistant cells survive and multiply. The infection becomes resistant.

This is why we use combination therapy for diseases like tuberculosis. If we use two drugs at once, the chance of a cell having resistance to both is the product of the individual chances. If each resistance mutation happens in 1 in 10⁸ cells, the chance of having both is 1 in 10¹⁶—extremely unlikely. Combination therapy is why TB is still treatable despite drug resistance.

image.alt: RNA polymerase with rifampicin binding site; mutation changes binding site, drug no longer works
image.caption: Rifampicin resistance: Mutation in RNA polymerase gene → amino acid change → drug no longer binds → bacteria survive. Random mutation pre-exists in population; selection spreads it.

classifications.value: Understanding horizontal gene transfer explains how bacteria get new genes from other bacteria, including antibiotic resistance.
characteristics: Transformation: Uptake of free DNA from environment. Requires competence (ability to take up DNA). Some bacteria are naturally competent (Streptococcus, Bacillus); others can be made competent in lab.

Transduction: DNA transferred by viruses (bacteriophages). Generalized transduction: virus picks up random bacterial DNA. Specialized transduction: virus picks up specific genes near where it inserted.
Conjugation: Direct cell-to-cell transfer via pilus. Donor cell copies plasmid and sends copy to recipient. F plasmid (fertility) makes cells donors; R plasmids carry resistance genes.
Frequency: Conjugation is most important in clinical settings. Can transfer between different species.
classifications.behavior:
Bacteria can get new genes from other bacteria without being their offspring. This is called horizontal gene transfer. It is one of the reasons bacteria can adapt so quickly—they can acquire ready-made solutions instead of waiting for mutations.

Transformation: Some bacteria can take up DNA from their environment. If a bacterium dies and breaks open, its DNA spills out. A living bacterium can grab this DNA and pull it inside. If the DNA has a useful gene (like antibiotic resistance), the bacterium can incorporate it into its own chromosome. This is how Griffith discovered that DNA is the genetic material in 1928. He killed virulent bacteria and mixed them with live harmless bacteria. The harmless bacteria became virulent—they had taken up DNA from the dead cells.

Transduction: Bacteriophages are viruses that infect bacteria. When they reproduce inside a bacterium, they make many copies of themselves. Sometimes, instead of packing viral DNA, they accidentally pack a piece of bacterial DNA. When this virus infects another bacterium, it injects that bacterial DNA. The new bacterium gets new genes.

Conjugation: Some bacteria have a special plasmid called the F plasmid (fertility plasmid). Bacteria with F plasmid grow a tube called a pilus. They attach to a bacterium without the plasmid and pull it close. Then they copy the plasmid and send the copy through the tube. The recipient now has the plasmid and becomes a donor itself. This is how antibiotic resistance spreads—resistance genes are often on R plasmids (resistance plasmids), which can be transferred by conjugation.

examples.explanation:
Conjugation is like bacterial sex, but it is not for reproduction—it is for sharing genes. A bacterium with an F plasmid (donor) grows a pilus—a hollow tube. It attaches to a recipient bacterium (without the plasmid) and pulls it close. Then it copies the plasmid and sends the copy through the tube. The recipient now has the plasmid and becomes a donor itself.

R plasmids carry antibiotic resistance genes. They often have multiple resistance genes—sometimes five or more. A single plasmid can make a bacterium resistant to many different antibiotics. And because R plasmids can be transferred by conjugation, resistance spreads quickly through bacterial populations.

In a hospital, a patient with a resistant E. coli infection can pass the bacteria to others. But even if they do not, the resistant E. coli can transfer its R plasmid to other E. coli in the patient's gut. The resistance can spread without the bacteria themselves spreading. This is why infection control is so important—and why we need to use antibiotics carefully.

image.alt: Conjugation: donor with pilus attaching to recipient, plasmid DNA transferring through tube
image.caption: Conjugation: Donor with F plasmid grows pilus → attaches to recipient → copies plasmid → sends copy → recipient becomes donor. R plasmids spread resistance this way.

principles.principle: Horizontal Gene Transfer Speeds Up Evolution
principles.statement: Bacteria can get new genes from other bacteria through horizontal gene transfer. This is much faster than waiting for mutations. It is why antibiotic resistance spreads so quickly.
principles.application:
Mutations are slow. A helpful mutation happens in about 1 in 100 million cells. But if a bacterium already has a resistance gene on a plasmid, it can share it with millions of neighbors in hours. Conjugation can transfer a plasmid from one bacterium to another in minutes. Within a day, a whole population can become resistant.

This is why antibiotic resistance is a crisis. A resistant bacterium in a hospital can spread its resistance genes to many other bacteria, even different species. E. coli can give a resistance plasmid to Salmonella, to Klebsiella, to Pseudomonas. The same plasmid can carry resistance to multiple antibiotics.

Horizontal gene transfer also spreads virulence genes. The bacteria that cause cholera and diphtheria got their toxin genes from viruses (transduction). Some bacteria got their ability to cause disease from plasmids. This is how harmless bacteria can become pathogens.
principles.example: NDM-1 is an enzyme that breaks down carbapenems, which are last-resort antibiotics. The gene for NDM-1 is on a plasmid. In 2008, it was found in India. By 2010, it had spread to bacteria all over the world. It is now found in E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterobacter, and many other species. The plasmid carries multiple resistance genes, making bacteria resistant to almost all antibiotics. This spread happened through conjugation—the plasmid moved from one bacterium to another, across species boundaries, across continents.
principles.analogy: Horizontal gene transfer is like downloading an app instead of writing code from scratch. Mutations are like writing your own code—slow and error-prone. HGT is like downloading a ready-made app that does exactly what you need. It is fast and efficient.

image.alt: Comparison: mutation takes generations; HGT takes minutes to transfer plasmid
image.caption: Evolution speed: Mutation (slow, generations) vs. HGT (fast, minutes). HGT spreads resistance genes across populations and species rapidly.

principles.principle: Mutations Are Random, Selection Is Not
principles.statement: Mutations happen by chance. They are not caused by the environment. But when the environment changes (like when antibiotics are present), the mutations that help bacteria survive are selected.
principles.application:
Bacteria do not "know" they need resistance. They do not make mutations on purpose. Mutations happen randomly as DNA is copied. Most are harmful. A few are helpful. When the environment changes, the helpful mutations become important.

If there are no antibiotics, a mutation that gives resistance might not help or might even hurt (resistance genes can slow growth). But when antibiotics are present, that mutation becomes very valuable. The resistant bacteria survive and multiply. The sensitive ones die. This is natural selection.

This is why we use antibiotics carefully. If we use them too much, we select for resistant bacteria. The resistant ones become more common. Eventually, the antibiotics stop working.
principles.example: Before penicillin was used, there were already rare bacteria with mutations that made them resistant. These mutations were not helpful (there was no penicillin), so they stayed rare. When penicillin was introduced, the resistant bacteria had a huge advantage. They survived and multiplied. Within a few years, resistant strains became common. The same thing happened with every new antibiotic.
principles.analogy: Mutations are like lottery tickets. Most are worthless. A few are winners. The environment (antibiotics) is like the lottery drawing. If the drawing happens (antibiotics are used), the winning tickets (resistant bacteria) get paid (survive and multiply). If there is no drawing, the tickets are worthless.

image.alt: Random mutations, then antibiotic selects resistant mutants
image.caption: Mutations are random; selection is not. Antibiotic pressure selects resistant mutants, which become dominant. Using antibiotics creates selection for resistance.

principles.statement: Bacteria have sophisticated systems to fix DNA mistakes. Without repair, mutation rates would be thousands of times higher, and most bacteria would die.
principles.application:
Copying DNA is not perfect. Without proofreading, the error rate would be about 1 mistake in 100,000 bases. That would be 40 mistakes per E. coli chromosome each time it copied. Most would be harmful. The cell would quickly accumulate deadly mutations.

Proofreading fixes most mistakes as they happen. Mismatch repair catches mistakes that get through. Together, they reduce the error rate to about 1 mistake in 10 billion bases. That is one mistake per 1,000 cells per generation. This is enough to allow evolution but not so many that the cell dies.

If the repair systems are broken, the mutation rate skyrockets. These hypermutators are often found in chronic infections, where they can evolve resistance faster. But they also accumulate harmful mutations, so they are rare in healthy environments.
principles.example: In cystic fibrosis patients, Pseudomonas aeruginosa often becomes a hypermutator. The bacteria lose their mismatch repair system. Their mutation rate goes up 100-fold. This helps them evolve resistance to antibiotics and adapt to the lung environment. But it also makes them accumulate harmful mutations. They are stuck in the patient—they cannot survive outside. Hypermutators are a trade-off: faster adaptation in the short term, but long-term decline.
principles.analogy: DNA repair is like a spell-checker. Without it, your writing would be full of typos. With it, most errors are fixed. But a few still get through. Those few are mutations—the typos that survive. They can be useful or harmful.

image.alt: DNA repair mechanisms: proofreading during replication, mismatch repair after replication
image.caption: DNA repair: Proofreading (during replication) fixes most errors. Mismatch repair (after replication) fixes remaining errors. Together, error rate ~1 in 10¹⁰ bases. Hypermutators lack mismatch repair.

components.definition: Bacterial genetics has three key components: the chromosome (essential genes), plasmids (extra genes), and horizontal gene transfer (sharing genes).
components.interpretation: Bacteria adapt by two main mechanisms: mutations (changing their own genes) and horizontal gene transfer (getting new genes from others). Mutations are slow but can create new traits. HGT is fast and can bring in ready-made traits. Together, they make bacteria extremely adaptable.

image.alt: Diagram showing chromosome, plasmids, and three HGT mechanisms
image.caption: Genetic components: Chromosome (essential), Plasmids (extra), HGT (transformation, transduction, conjugation). Bacteria use both mutations and HGT to adapt.

categories.description: CRISPR is a bacterial defense system against viruses. It remembers past infections and can destroy viral DNA if the same virus attacks again.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
CRISPR stands for Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats. It is a bacterial immune system. When a virus infects a bacterium, the bacterium can capture a piece of the viral DNA and insert it into its own DNA. This is like a wanted poster. If the same virus attacks again, the bacterium makes RNA copies of the stored viral DNA. These RNA guide a protein (Cas) to the viral DNA, which is cut and destroyed.

CRISPR is a natural system, but scientists have turned it into a powerful tool. By designing a guide RNA, we can direct Cas to cut any DNA we want. This allows us to edit genes—cut out bad genes, insert new ones, or turn genes on or off. CRISPR is used in research, medicine, and agriculture.

Bacteria without CRISPR are more likely to pick up viruses—and also more likely to pick up plasmids with antibiotic resistance. Some bacteria use CRISPR to block conjugation. This is an arms race: bacteria evolve CRISPR to block plasmids; plasmids evolve ways to block CRISPR.

examples.process: How CRISPR Protects Bacteria from Viruses
examples.explanation:
When a virus infects a bacterium, the bacterium can capture a piece of the viral DNA and insert it into the CRISPR array. This array is a region of the bacterial genome that stores viral DNA snippets. Each snippet is separated by repeats.

When the same virus attacks again, the bacterium makes RNA copies of the stored snippets. These RNAs bind to a protein called Cas (CRISPR-associated). The Cas-RNA complex searches for viral DNA that matches the RNA. When it finds a match, it cuts the viral DNA. The virus is destroyed.

This system remembers past infections. It is like an immune system. Bacteria can pass this memory to their offspring. Over generations, a bacterial population can build up immunity to many viruses.

Scientists have adapted this system for genome editing. By designing a guide RNA, we can direct Cas to cut any DNA sequence. This allows us to knock out genes, insert new genes, or make precise changes. CRISPR is now used in labs around the world to study gene function, make disease models, and develop therapies.

image.alt: CRISPR system: virus DNA captured, stored in CRISPR array, used to guide Cas to cut viral DNA
image.caption: CRISPR: Bacteria capture viral DNA → store in CRISPR array → make RNA guides → Cas protein cuts matching viral DNA. Adaptive immunity against viruses. Adapted for genome editing.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Tracing Antibiotic Resistance Spread

worked_examples.title: How Did Resistance Spread in a Hospital?
worked_examples.problem: A hospital sees an increase in carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae. Investigation shows: (a) The bacteria are from different strains (different genetic fingerprints). (b) They all carry the same resistance gene (blaKPC) on the same type of plasmid. (c) The plasmid can transfer between bacteria by conjugation. (d) Patients were not in direct contact. (1) How did the resistance spread? (2) What evidence supports your answer? (3) What could the hospital do to stop further spread?

step_by_step.calculation: Different strains → not the same bacterium spreading. Same plasmid in different strains → plasmid moved between strains. Plasmid can transfer by conjugation → likely mechanism. No direct patient contact → spread through environment (hands, equipment) or through carriers.

Conjugation: The resistant bacteria (donors) transferred the plasmid to sensitive bacteria (recipients). Because the plasmid can move between different strains, the resistance spread through the bacterial population without the bacteria themselves spreading. This is horizontal gene transfer.

step_by_step.explanation: Explain why other mechanisms are less likely.
step_by_step.calculation: Mutation: Different strains would need to independently develop the same rare mutation—unlikely. Transformation: K. pneumoniae is not naturally competent. Transduction: No phage evidence; plasmids are large, not typical for phages. Conjugation best fits the data.

Control: (1) Screen patients for carriers (rectal swabs). (2) Isolate carriers to prevent spread. (3) Improve hand hygiene and equipment cleaning. (4) Use antibiotics carefully to reduce selection pressure. (5) Consider decolonization (reducing bacteria in carriers). Conjugation requires cell contact; preventing contact reduces transfer.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Resistance spread by conjugation—plasmid transfer between different strains. (2) Evidence: different strains, same plasmid, plasmid is conjugative. (3) Control: screening, isolation, hygiene, antibiotic stewardship. This shows how horizontal gene transfer spreads resistance, and how to stop it.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Horizontal gene transfer (conjugation) spreads resistance between strains. To stop it, prevent contact between resistant and sensitive bacteria. Screen and isolate carriers. Use antibiotics carefully to reduce selection.

sections.introduction: Bacteria can get new genes from other bacteria in three ways. Each has different requirements and outcomes.

process_flow.description: Bacteria take up DNA from their environment.
process_flow.mechanism: Some bacteria are naturally competent (able to take up DNA). They bind DNA, pull it in, and incorporate it into their chromosome. In the lab, we can make E. coli competent by treating with calcium chloride or electric shock.
process_flow.result: Recipient gets new genes from dead bacteria.

image.alt: Free DNA taken up by competent bacterium, incorporated into chromosome
image.caption: Transformation: Competent bacterium takes up free DNA → incorporates into chromosome → new genes.

process_flow.description: Bacteriophages carry bacterial DNA from one cell to another.
process_flow.mechanism: Phage infects donor, copies itself. Sometimes it packages bacterial DNA instead of viral DNA. When it infects a new cell, it injects that DNA. The DNA recombines into the recipient's chromosome.
process_flow.result: Recipient gets DNA from donor via phage.

image.alt: Phage picks up bacterial DNA, injects into new bacterium
image.caption: Transduction: Phage infects donor → packages bacterial DNA → infects recipient → DNA incorporated. Phage can transfer genes between bacteria.

process_flow.description: Donor cell transfers DNA directly to recipient through a pilus.
process_flow.mechanism: Donor with F plasmid grows pilus, attaches to recipient. Copies plasmid and sends copy through the pilus. Recipient becomes donor. R plasmids (resistance) transfer this way.
process_flow.result: Recipient gets plasmid (often carrying resistance genes).

image.alt: Donor with pilus attaching to recipient, plasmid DNA transferring
image.caption: Conjugation: Donor (with plasmid) extends pilus → attaches to recipient → copies plasmid → sends copy → recipient becomes donor. Major route for antibiotic resistance spread.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: The Antibiotic Resistance Crisis
sections.definition: Antibiotic resistance is one of the biggest threats to modern medicine. It is driven by bacterial genetics—mutations and horizontal gene transfer—and fueled by overuse of antibiotics.

process_types.energy_profile: Resistance genes on plasmids spread by conjugation. Plasmids carry multiple resistance genes, making bacteria resistant to many drugs.
process_types.direction: Resistant bacteria → transfer plasmid → sensitive bacteria become resistant → more resistant bacteria
process_types.function: Understand how resistance spreads to design better infection control.
goals: Stop spread of resistant bacteria (isolation, hygiene)
Stop spread of resistance genes (antibiotic stewardship)
process_types.energy_source: Resistance genes cost energy to maintain; bacteria lose them if not needed. Antibiotics create selection pressure that maintains them.

primary_example.explanation:
NDM-1 is an enzyme that breaks down carbapenems—last-resort antibiotics. The gene for NDM-1 is on a plasmid. It was first found in India in 2008. Within a few years, it had spread to bacteria all over the world.

The NDM-1 plasmid carries multiple resistance genes. Bacteria with this plasmid are resistant to almost all antibiotics. They are called superbugs. Infections with these bacteria are very hard to treat.

The plasmid spreads by conjugation. It has moved from Klebsiella to E. coli to Enterobacter to many other species. It has also moved from India to Europe, North America, and everywhere else. This is a global problem.

To stop it, we need to: (1) Use antibiotics carefully—stop selecting for resistant bacteria. (2) Improve infection control—stop resistant bacteria from spreading in hospitals. (3) Develop new antibiotics—so we have something to use when old ones fail. (4) Develop new approaches—like phage therapy or CRISPR to target resistance genes.

image.alt: NDM-1 plasmid structure, global spread map
image.caption: NDM-1: Carbapenemase on conjugative plasmid. Spread from India to worldwide in a few years. Carries multiple resistance genes. Major public health threat.

image.alt: Cycle: antibiotic use → selection → resistant bacteria spread → more antibiotics used
image.caption: Resistance cycle: Antibiotic use selects resistant bacteria → resistant bacteria spread (HGT) → more infections require antibiotics → more selection. Need to break the cycle.

process_types.energy_profile: Combination therapy, stewardship, and infection control reduce selection and spread.
process_types.direction: Overuse → resistance → crisis → stewardship + new drugs + infection control → sustainable use
goals: Use combination therapy to prevent resistance
process_types.energy_source: Antibiotic development requires research investment; stewardship requires behavioral change.

primary_example.explanation:
Tuberculosis is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. It grows slowly, and treatment takes months. If a single drug is used, resistance develops quickly.

The standard treatment uses four drugs at once: isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol. The chance of a bacterium having resistance to all four is extremely low. Combination therapy works even if the patient already has some resistant bacteria.

This is why TB treatment takes so long (6-9 months) and uses multiple drugs. It is designed to prevent resistance. If a patient stops early, resistant bacteria can grow back. This is why directly observed therapy (watching patients take their pills) is used—to make sure they finish the course.

The same principle applies to HIV (combination antiretroviral therapy) and other infections. Using multiple drugs at once is the best way to prevent resistance.

image.alt: Four drugs used together for TB, chance of resistance to all is very low
image.caption: Combination therapy: Use multiple drugs to reduce resistance risk. TB treatment uses 4 drugs for 6 months. Chance of resistance to all is extremely low. Prevents treatment failure.

image.alt: Antibiotic stewardship: use right drug, right dose, right duration
image.caption: Antibiotic stewardship: Use antibiotics only when needed, use the narrowest spectrum, use the right dose, finish the course. Reduces selection for resistance.

sections.relationship: Resistance spreads through bacterial genetics—mutations create resistance genes; horizontal gene transfer spreads them. We combat it with stewardship (reduce selection), infection control (stop spread), and new drugs (treat resistant infections).

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding bacterial genetics. Knowing these terms will help you describe how bacteria store, copy, and share genetic information.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in bacterial genetics. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss bacterial evolution, antibiotic resistance, and genetic engineering with confidence.

image.alt: A diagram with key terms: chromosome, plasmid, mutation, transformation, transduction, conjugation
image.caption: Key genetics terms: Knowing these helps you understand how bacteria evolve and share genes.

key_properties.explanation: The main DNA molecule in bacteria. Circular, about 4-5 million base pairs. Contains essential genes. Located in the nucleoid (no nucleus).
A small, circular DNA molecule separate from the chromosome. Carries non-essential genes that can be helpful (antibiotic resistance, toxins). Can be transferred between bacteria.
A change in the DNA sequence. Can be harmful, neutral, or helpful. Helpful mutations can spread by natural selection. Source of new traits.
Transfer of genes between bacteria that are not parent and offspring. Three types: transformation, transduction, conjugation. Major way antibiotic resistance spreads.
Uptake of free DNA from the environment. Some bacteria are naturally competent; others can be made competent in the lab. Used in genetic engineering.
Transfer of DNA by viruses (bacteriophages). Phage picks up bacterial DNA and injects it into a new cell. Can transfer genes between different species.
Direct transfer of DNA from one bacterium to another through a pilus. Donor transfers plasmid to recipient. Major mechanism for spreading antibiotic resistance.
Fertility plasmid. Makes bacteria donors. Carries genes for pilus formation and DNA transfer. Basis for conjugation.
Resistance plasmid. Carries genes for antibiotic resistance. Often transferable by conjugation. Major vehicle for spreading resistance.
Copying DNA. Starts at oriC. Two replication forks move in opposite directions. Takes about 40 minutes in E. coli.
Origin of replication. The specific spot on the bacterial chromosome where replication begins.
DNA polymerase checks each base as it is added. If wrong, it removes it and tries again. Catches most errors during replication.
System that fixes errors missed by proofreading. Scans DNA after replication, cuts out mismatched bases, and fills in correctly. Reduces error rate 100-1000×.
Response to DNA damage. Stops cell division, activates error-prone polymerases. Increases mutation rate. Helps survival but can create resistance.
Bacterial immune system. Stores DNA from past viral infections. Uses stored sequences to recognize and destroy viral DNA if it attacks again. Adapted for genome editing.
A gene that makes bacteria resistant to an antibiotic. Often on plasmids. Spreads by horizontal gene transfer.
When an antibiotic kills sensitive bacteria, resistant ones survive and multiply. This is natural selection. Antibiotic use creates selection for resistance.
Using multiple antibiotics at once. Reduces risk of resistance because bacteria need multiple mutations to resist all drugs. Used for TB, HIV, and other infections.

summary:
Bacteria have a single circular chromosome that holds their essential genes. They may also have plasmids—small DNA circles with extra genes like antibiotic resistance. Before dividing, they copy their DNA with high accuracy. Mistakes (mutations) happen rarely, but when they do, they can create new traits.

Bacteria share genes through horizontal gene transfer. Transformation: taking up free DNA from the environment. Transduction: DNA carried by viruses. Conjugation: direct transfer through a pilus. Conjugation is the most important mechanism for spreading antibiotic resistance—a resistant bacterium can give a copy of its resistance plasmid to a sensitive neighbor in minutes.

Mutations are random, but selection is not. When antibiotics are used, resistant bacteria survive and multiply. This is why overuse of antibiotics drives resistance. Combination therapy (using multiple drugs) reduces the risk of resistance because bacteria need multiple mutations to escape all the drugs.

CRISPR is a bacterial immune system that remembers past viral infections. It uses stored viral DNA to recognize and destroy attacking viruses. Scientists have adapted CRISPR for genome editing—a powerful tool for research and medicine.

Understanding bacterial genetics helps us understand how bacteria evolve, how antibiotic resistance spreads, and how we can fight it. It also helps us use bacteria for good—in biotechnology, we use plasmids to make insulin, human growth hormone, and other medicines.
